metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the AC4 completeness test: e.g., convert the registry comment table into a machine-readable Python dict/list (single source of truth), then assert that every entry in that dict has a horizon field and that each named surface emits a warning in isolation — eliminating the circular double-maintenance problem.
findings.finding: AC4 proposes a 'test/CI check asserts new aliases get a registry row', but the plan gives no specification of what the test actually asserts. The list of 'deprecated surfaces' is not a computable set in the codebase (no machine-readable manifest exists); without a concrete mechanism, the test will either be a tautological list-in-two-places assertion or will only catch the surfaces the author remembers to add — making the completeness guarantee hollow.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — '- [ ] A test/CI check asserts new aliases get a registry row'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the prose comment table at `config.py:444-458` with a small structured constant (e.g., `_DEPRECATION_REGISTRY: list[DeprecatedSurface]` where `DeprecatedSurface` is a NamedTuple with `kind`, `name`, `canonical`, `remove_in` fields). The CI test then asserts `len(_DEPRECATION_REGISTRY) >= N` and that each entry has a non-empty `remove_in`. This makes the registry machine-readable and the completeness test non-circular.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify HOW the AC4 test/CI check will enforce registry completeness against the comment-block table — either convert the deprecation-removal registry to a machine-readable Python data structure (e.g. a `_DEPRECATION_REGISTRY` list in `config.py`) that a test can import and cross-check against `_LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES` and other deprecated surfaces, or describe the exact static-analysis/grep mechanism and what it will assert.
findings.finding: AC4 ('A test/CI check asserts new aliases get a registry row') is mechanically unachievable as specified because the registry is a pure doc-comment block (config.py:444-452), not a machine-readable data structure — there is no specified mechanism by which a test can introspect it to enforce completeness.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the doc-comment table at config.py:444-452 with a machine-readable list such as:
```python
_DEPRECATION_REGISTRY = [
    # (kind, alias, canonical, remove_in)
    ('env', 'REBAR_PUSH', 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH', 'v1.0.0'),
    ...
]
```
A test can then import `_DEPRECATION_REGISTRY` and assert every key in `_LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES` has a corresponding entry, making AC4 a real enforcing check rather than a documentation aspiration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Remediation step (step 4 or sub-step of step 1) describing HOW the CI/test check for registry completeness will be implemented — e.g., a pytest test that introspects all DeprecationWarning-emitting surfaces and asserts each has a registry row — so AC4 maps to described work.
findings.finding: AC4 ('A test/CI check asserts new aliases get a registry row') has no corresponding described work in any of the three Remediation steps. It is an orphan criterion: the plan body describes adding registry rows (step 1), emitting warnings (step 2), and fixing log wording (step 3), but never describes writing or modifying a test or CI check. An implementer following the Remediation has no guidance on how to satisfy this criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step such as: 'Step 4: Add a pytest test (e.g., tests/test_deprecation_registry.py) that enumerates all call sites emitting DeprecationWarning (via AST scan or a canonical import) and asserts each is present in the registry table at config.py:444-458. Wire it into CI as a required check.' This makes AC4 traceable.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or test requirement) explicitly requiring that each of the four newly-warned surfaces (edit_ticket(tags=), flat-conf reader, --no-sync, MCP list_epics) is exercised by a test that asserts a DeprecationWarning (correct category and message) is raised — not just that warning code was added.
findings.finding: The four surfaces newly required to emit DeprecationWarning (edit_ticket(tags=), flat .rebar/config.conf reader, --no-sync, MCP list_epics) have no corresponding test AC — changed behavior (silent → warning) with no modify/add-test work specified.
findings.location: Remediation item 2 / AC line 2: 'Every scheduled-removal surface emits a DeprecationWarning in this release'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'Unit/integration tests assert that calling edit_ticket(tags=...), reading a flat .rebar/config.conf, passing --no-sync, and invoking the MCP list_epics tool each raises a DeprecationWarning with a message referencing the removal horizon.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Step 2 whether `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`'s existing `logger.warning()` in `llm/config.py:369` satisfies the 'emit a DeprecationWarning' requirement, or whether it also needs conversion to `warnings.warn(..., DeprecationWarning)` like the other Step 2 targets.
findings.finding: The plan adds `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` as a row in the core `config.py` registry comment but the alias lives in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` — a separate module that already emits `logger.warning()` but not `warnings.warn(DeprecationWarning)`. The plan conflates 'add a registry row' (doc hygiene) with 'ensure DeprecationWarning is emitted' (Step 2) without clarifying which module's warning mechanism needs updating and whether the logger-based warning in llm/config.py satisfies Step 2's requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct Remediation step 1: `rebar.list_epics()` lib already has a registry row at `config.py:452` and already emits `DeprecationWarning`; the real gap is the MCP *tool wrapper* (`_mcp_reads.py:246-247`) which suppresses that warning — add a registry row for it and remove the `warnings.simplefilter('ignore')` suppression.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'rebar.list_epics()' (the lib function) is a registry gap and needs a new row ('Add rows for the 4 uncatalogued deprecated surfaces (MCP list_epics...)'). Reading config.py:452 confirms 'lib rebar.list_epics() → list_tickets(ticket_type='epic') v1.0.0' is already registered. The actual uncatalogued surface is the MCP *tool wrapper* in _mcp_reads.py:234-252, which suppresses the lib's DeprecationWarning. The plan conflates the lib function (already registered, already emits DeprecationWarning in _lib_reads.py:299-305) with the MCP tool wrapper (unregistered, silences the warning).
findings.location: Finding section 'MCP list_epics tool src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py:239-252' / Remediation step 1
findings.suggested_fix: Change Remediation step 1 to: 'Add a registry row for the MCP list_epics *tool wrapper* (distinct from the already-registered lib function); in step 2, remove the warnings.simplefilter("ignore", DeprecationWarning) at _mcp_reads.py:247 so the lib's warning propagates to MCP callers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify warning mechanism for each surface type: for library/CLI surfaces use `warnings.warn(..., DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)` combined with an explicit `warnings.simplefilter('always', DeprecationWarning)` guard or a `logger.warning` fallback; for MCP surfaces, emit a structured MCP-level deprecation notice (e.g., in the tool's response metadata or a logged server-side warning) rather than relying on a Python DeprecationWarning.
findings.finding: The plan directs that scheduled-removal surfaces 'emit a DeprecationWarning in this release', but Python's default warning filter silences DeprecationWarnings in non-development environments (since Python 3.2, DeprecationWarning is ignored unless the code is in __main__ or a test runner). Users running rebar as a library or in a production shell script will receive no signal before v1.0.0 removal. The MCP list_epics context is especially at risk: 'stop suppressing' the DeprecationWarning may still not surface it through the MCP transport layer to the end user.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct Remediation step 1: `edit_ticket(tags=)` already has a registry row at `config.py:451`; remove it from the 'add rows' list and update the count — the actual registry gaps are `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`, `--verdict-hash`, `lock_backend="file"`, and CLI `--no-sync`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'edit_ticket(tags=)' is missing from the registry ('registry gaps' citation: '_commands/composer.py:345-358 (no warning)'). Reading config.py:451 confirms 'lib edit_ticket(tags=…) → set_tags/add_tags/remove_tags v1.0.0' is already present as a registry row. The real gap is only the missing runtime warning signal, not a missing registry row. Remediation step 1 ('Add rows for the 4 uncatalogued deprecated surfaces') is wrong to include edit_ticket(tags=) in the set of surfaces needing a new row.
findings.location: Finding section 'Silent scheduled removals: edit_ticket(tags=) _commands/composer.py:345-358 (no warning)' / Remediation step 1
findings.suggested_fix: In Remediation step 1, replace 'edit_ticket(tags=)' with 'CLI --no-sync alias (reads_cli.py:624)' in the list of surfaces needing new registry rows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct Remediation step 1 and AC-1: `flat .rebar/config.conf reader` already has a registry row at `config.py:450`; remove it from the 'add rows' list and note its only gap is the missing `DeprecationWarning` call inside `_read_legacy_conf()`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'flat .rebar/config.conf reader config.py:303,380' is a registry gap (listed under 'Silent scheduled removals'). Reading config.py:450 shows 'cfg flat .rebar/config.conf reader → rebar.toml / [tool.rebar] pyproject v1.0.0' is already present as a registry row. The genuine gap for this surface is only the missing runtime DeprecationWarning when _read_legacy_conf() is invoked — the registry entry exists.
findings.location: Finding section 'Silent scheduled removals: flat .rebar/config.conf reader config.py:303,380' / Remediation step 1
findings.suggested_fix: Move flat .rebar/config.conf reader from step 1 ('add registry rows') to step 2 ('ensure runtime DeprecationWarning') only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add CLI `--no-sync` alias (`reads_cli.py:624`) explicitly to Remediation step 1's list of surfaces needing both a new registry row and a runtime `DeprecationWarning`; update the count from 4 to the correct number of genuine registry gaps.
findings.finding: The plan's 'silent scheduled removals' list omits CLI --no-sync alias (reads_cli.py:624-627), which is genuinely absent from the registry table and emits no warning. The plan correctly notes it in the citations but does not explicitly name it in Remediation step 1's list of surfaces needing new registry rows — instead, the plan lists 4 surfaces (MCP list_epics, REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS, --verdict-hash, lock_backend='file'). Given that edit_ticket(tags=) and flat-conf-reader are falsely listed as registry gaps, and --no-sync is genuinely missing, the corrected list of true registry gaps is: REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS, --verdict-hash, lock_backend='file', and CLI --no-sync.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the registry header comment (config.py ~line 429) and/or CLAUDE.md to define the expanded scope of 'deprecated user-facing surface' (MCP tools, CLI flags, cfg values, env vars, Python APIs) and the rule that every such surface must have a registry row — so the CI check has a documented, unambiguous contract to enforce.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new CI-enforced contract ('registry is genuinely complete') but does not specify where the definition of 'user-facing deprecated surface' and the rules for adding a registry row will be documented, leaving future contributors without guidance on scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'A test/CI check asserts new aliases get a registry row'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief prose block at the top of the registry table (and a pointer in CLAUDE.md) listing the surface categories now covered: env aliases, cfg keys, CLI flags/options, MCP tool names, cfg value enumerations. State the rule: 'Any deprecated surface in these categories MUST have a row added in the same PR that introduces the deprecation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE step for registry rows: specify who removes a row when its scheduled horizon arrives (e.g., same PR that deletes the surface must also delete its registry row), and extend the CI check to assert no registry row references a non-existent surface/symbol.
findings.finding: The registry's RETIRE lifecycle for scheduled-removal rows is unspecified. Once a surface reaches its removal horizon and is deleted, nothing in the plan (including the proposed CI check) mandates or even describes removing the now-stale registry row. The CI check asserts only that new aliases obtain rows, leaving expired rows to accumulate indefinitely and undermining the registry's claimed completeness.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Remediation sub-step: 'When a deprecated surface is deleted at its horizon, its registry row MUST be removed in the same PR. Extend the CI check to enumerate registry row targets and assert each referenced symbol/env-var/CLI flag still exists in the codebase.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider extracting the permanent-rename wording fix (Remediation step 3 / AC line 3) into a child ticket, since it has a distinct reason-to-change (stable-alias messaging correctness) and is independently valuable and releasable from the scheduled-removal signalling work.
findings.finding: Goal 3 (reconcile permanent-rename wording) is independently valuable, testable, and releasable from Goals 1+2, constituting a separate reason-to-change (stable-feature log messaging vs. scheduled-removal signalling), yet is bundled into the same ticket without a child decomposition.
findings.location: Remediation item 3 / AC line 3: 'The permanent-rename env aliases' log wording no longer claims deprecated'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
